blurb: Lavish, fragrant late-spring blooms on a clump that can outlive the gardener.
spread: Clumping — long-lived & slow; resents being moved
seasons: Big fragrant flowers late spring–early summer; lush green summer; bronze fall; dies to ground in winter
wildlife: Bees (the ants on buds are harmless)
toxic: Toxic to dogs, cats & horses if eaten

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8 — exceptionally winter-hardy on the Boulder Front Range (zone 5b–6a). The top growth dies to the ground after frost; the roots survive unaided. No lifting or special protection is needed. Peonies actually require a cold dormant period to set buds, so Colorado winters are an asset, not a threat.
care.sun: Full sun is ideal — at least 6 hours a day. Afternoon shade is tolerated and can extend bloom in heat, but fewer than 4–5 hours causes sparse flowering and floppy stems. The intense high-altitude sun on the Front Range suits them well.
care.soil: Rich, fertile, well-drained soil with medium moisture. Work compost into the planting hole before setting the root. Front Range clay-loam is acceptable with good drainage — peonies sit in the same spot for decades, so take time to prepare the hole properly. They dislike standing water, especially over winter.
care.water: Moderate — keep soil evenly moist during the growing season, especially in spring as buds form. Once established, deep weekly watering through dry spells is enough; the semi-arid Front Range climate (~15–18 in/year) requires supplemental irrigation in summer. Ease off in fall to encourage hardening.
care.depth: Plant eyes (red growth buds on the crown) no deeper than 1 in below the soil surface — CSU Extension is explicit: no more than one inch. This is the single most common reason peonies fail to bloom. If planted too deep, the plant produces only foliage — dig and re-set the crown shallower rather than waiting years for it to correct itself.
care.spacing: Space 3–4 ft apart — each clump matures to 2–3 ft wide and equally tall. Crowding limits airflow and invites botrytis. Give them permanent real estate; they resent transplanting and can outlive the gardener once settled.
care.bloom: Late May to early June on the Front Range, with individual flowers lasting roughly 7–10 days. Planting a mix of early, midseason, and late cultivars extends the display to about 6 weeks. Expect no reliable blooms for the first 2–3 years after planting; full performance arrives around years 3–5.
care.feeding: Apply a balanced or low-nitrogen fertilizer (e.g. 5-10-10) in early spring as shoots emerge, and again after bloom. Excess nitrogen promotes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Top-dress with compost every few years to maintain soil organic matter; the alkaline Front Range soil rarely needs pH amendment.
care.maintenance: Stake clumps early in spring — the large, heavy flowers collect rainwater and topple without support; wire peony rings set over emerging growth are the easiest solution. Remove spent flower heads by snipping just behind the head, leaving the stem and leaves to feed the crown. Cut all foliage to the ground in fall after frost and remove it from the garden (don't compost) to break the botrytis cycle. Leave established clumps undisturbed indefinitely — division is rarely needed and often sets the plant back by years.
care.troubles: Generally robust. Botrytis (gray mold) is the main disease threat: stems blacken and wilt, usually in cool, wet springs. Remove affected tissue immediately, improve airflow, and avoid overhead watering. Powdery mildew can appear in late summer but is cosmetic. Ants on buds are harmless — they're after nectar and do not help or hinder bloom opening. Thrips, scale, and Phytophthora blight are occasional; good drainage and sanitation prevent most problems.
care.companions: Bearded iris (same bloom window, similar drainage needs), catmint, alliums, salvia, and old roses. The large summer foliage pairs well with airy grasses like little bluestem. Plant late-emerging perennials (coneflower, Russian sage) nearby to fill the garden after peonies finish blooming.
